reflection.quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act, but a habit.
reflection.reading:
Thursday in mid-May is one of those days that used to feel like nothing. Not the weekend, not a fresh start, just the slow grind of a week that hadn't paid off yet. When I was drinking, days like this were the ones I most wanted to skip — clock out, crack one open, fast-forward to something more interesting. The problem was that most of life is Thursday. Most of life is the unglamorous middle of the week, the unfinished project, the dinner that needs cooking, the run you don't feel like going on.

The thing nobody tells you when you get sober is that you slowly start to like Thursdays. Not because they become exciting, but because you stop trying to escape them. You make the coffee. You return the text. You go to the meeting or the gym or the trail. The day stacks itself, one small honest action at a time, and at some point you look up and realize you've built something — a body that works, friendships that show up, a head that's quiet enough to think in.

Durant was right that we are what we repeat. Sobriety isn't a single dramatic decision. It's the boring stack of Thursdays you actually showed up for.
reflection.meditation: Today, let the ordinary be enough. Show up for the small things and trust that they add up.

affirmations: I am building a life out of the days I used to want to skip.
Today I have the clarity to choose my next action instead of react to my next feeling.
I am someone who shows up — for the work, the people, and myself.

gratitude.prayer: I'm grateful for a quiet Thursday and a clear head to meet it with. I'm grateful for the people who texted back, the body that still works, and the fact that I no longer have to negotiate with myself about what I drank last night. The ordinary stuff is the whole gift — I just couldn't see it before.
gratitude.closing: One more honest day. That's the whole thing.
